questionText: Which of the following is NOT a primitive data type in Java?
explanation: String is a class (reference type) in Java, not a primitive. The 8 primitive types are: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean.

questionText: What is the purpose of the 'final' keyword in Java?
optionB: To prevent modification — final variables can't be reassigned, final methods can't be overridden, final classes can't be extended
explanation: The 'final' keyword creates constants (variables), prevents method overriding, or prevents class inheritance. A final variable can only be assigned once.

questionText: What is the difference between == and .equals() in Java?
optionA: They are the same for all types
optionC: == is for strings; .equals() is for numbers
explanation: For objects, == checks if two references point to the same memory location. .equals() checks logical equality (content). For primitives, == compares values directly.

questionText: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList in Java?
optionA: ArrayList uses a doubly linked list internally
optionB: ArrayList uses a dynamic array (fast random access); LinkedList uses doubly linked nodes (fast insertion/deletion)
explanation: ArrayList provides O(1) random access but O(n) insertion/deletion in the middle. LinkedList provides O(1) insertion/deletion (with pointer) but O(n) random access.

questionText: What is a Java interface?
optionA: A class that can be instantiated
optionB: A contract that defines methods a class must implement
explanation: An interface defines a contract of abstract methods that implementing classes must provide. Since Java 8, interfaces can also have default and static methods. A class can implement multiple interfaces.

questionText: What is the Java Memory Model's distinction between Stack and Heap?
optionA: Stack stores objects; Heap stores primitives
optionB: Stack stores local variables and method calls; Heap stores objects and class instances
optionC: They are the same memory area
explanation: Stack memory stores local variables, method parameters, and call frames (LIFO, per-thread). Heap memory stores object instances and arrays (shared across threads, managed by GC).

questionText: What is the purpose of the 'volatile' keyword in Java?
optionB: To ensure that a variable's value is always read from main memory, ensuring visibility across threads
optionD: To mark a variable for garbage collection
explanation: The 'volatile' keyword ensures that reads and writes to a variable go directly to main memory, preventing thread-local caching. It provides visibility guarantees but not atomicity.

questionText: Which exception is thrown when you try to access an array element with an invalid index?
explanation: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException is thrown when you access an array with an index that is negative or greater than or equal to the array length.

questionText: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
optionA: Checked exceptions occur at runtime; unchecked at compile time
optionB: Checked exceptions must be caught or declared (compile-time); unchecked exceptions don't require explicit handling (runtime)
explanation: Checked exceptions (IOException, SQLException) must be caught or declared in the method signature. Unchecked exceptions (NullPointerException, ArithmeticException) extend RuntimeException and don't require explicit handling.

questionText: What is a lambda expression in Java?
optionB: A concise way to represent an anonymous function that implements a functional interface
optionD: A design pattern for database access
explanation: Lambda expressions (Java 8+) provide a concise syntax for implementing functional interfaces (interfaces with one abstract method). Example: (a, b) -> a + b. They enable functional programming in Java.

questionText: What is the purpose of the 'synchronized' keyword?
optionA: To make methods run in parallel
optionB: To ensure only one thread can access a method or block at a time
explanation: The 'synchronized' keyword provides mutual exclusion — only one thread can execute a synchronized method or block on the same object at a time, preventing race conditions.

questionText: What is method overloading in Java?
optionA: Redefining a parent class method in a child class
optionB: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists in the same class
explanation: Method overloading allows multiple methods with the same name but different parameter types, counts, or order. The compiler determines which version to call based on the arguments provided.

questionText: What is the difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?
optionB: HashMap is not thread-safe; ConcurrentHashMap provides thread-safety using fine-grained locking (segment/bucket level)
optionC: ConcurrentHashMap is slower in all cases
explanation: HashMap is not thread-safe. ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and limited concurrent writes using segment-level locking (Java 7) or CAS operations (Java 8+), providing better performance than Collections.synchronizedMap().

questionText: What is autoboxing in Java?
optionB: Automatic conversion between primitive types and their wrapper classes
explanation: Autoboxing is the automatic conversion between primitives and their wrapper classes (e.g., int to Integer). Unboxing is the reverse. Introduced in Java 5 to simplify code with collections.

questionText: What is the default value of a boolean variable in Java?
explanation: The default value of a boolean instance variable in Java is false. Local variables don't have default values and must be initialized before use.
